$comment: URL canonicalization conformance vectors for the AdCP RFC 9421 request-signing profile. Each case asserts the canonical @target-uri and @authority values a signer MUST compute from the as-received URL before emitting the signature base, and that a verifier MUST recompute before verifying. Independent of cryptographic signing — an SDK can run this set without keys, crypto, or a full verifier harness. Cited by docs/building/implementation/security.mdx §AdCP RFC 9421 profile, `@target-uri` canonicalization.

cases.name: scheme-lowercase
cases.rule: step 1: lowercase the scheme

cases.name: host-lowercase
cases.rule: step 2: lowercase the host

cases.name: idn-to-punycode
cases.rule: step 2: UTS-46 Nontransitional ToASCII → Punycode A-label
cases.$comment: Punycode of 'bücher' is 'bcher-kva' → ACE label 'xn--bcher-kva'. Verifiable against any UTS-46-conformant IDN tool.

cases.name: idn-mixed-case-to-punycode
cases.rule: step 2: UTS-46 case-folds non-ASCII before Punycode; naive ASCII-lowercase + Punycode diverges
cases.$comment: Pins UTS-46 Nontransitional behavior. A signer that lowercases to 'bücher' via locale-dependent ASCII lowercasing before ToASCII still produces the same A-label here because 'Ü' → 'ü' is a UTS-46 mapping, but implementations that skip UTS-46 entirely (e.g., raw idnaEncodeLabel) can diverge on other scripts — this case is the canary.

cases.name: ipv6-host-hex-lowercased
cases.rule: step 2: IPv6 brackets preserved; hex digits inside lowercased

cases.name: ipv6-host-with-port
cases.rule: step 2 + step 4: IPv6 brackets + non-default port preserved

cases.name: userinfo-stripped
cases.rule: step 3: strip userinfo

cases.name: default-port-https-stripped
cases.rule: step 4: strip :443 for https

cases.name: default-port-http-stripped
cases.rule: step 4: strip :80 for http

cases.name: non-default-port-preserved
cases.rule: step 4: preserve non-default ports

cases.name: dot-segment-collapsed
cases.rule: step 5: remove_dot_segments (RFC 3986 §5.2.4)

cases.name: double-dot-segment-collapsed
cases.rule: step 5: remove_dot_segments resolves /a/b/../c to /a/c

cases.name: consecutive-slashes-preserved
cases.rule: step 5: consecutive slashes preserved byte-for-byte (NOT collapsed)
cases.$comment: RFC 3986 does not mandate collapsing consecutive slashes. Preserving closes a path-confusion attack surface where the server routes /a//b differently from /a/b; deployments MUST disable slash-folding on signed routes (nginx merge_slashes off; Express: do not pre-normalize; Go http.ServeMux 1.22+: use explicit http.Handler).

cases.name: dot-segment-with-consecutive-slashes
cases.rule: step 5: remove_dot_segments + preserve consecutive slashes; /a/.//b → /a//b
cases.$comment: remove_dot_segments resolves /./ but does not collapse the following //. Pins the interaction explicitly — some parsers eagerly collapse // during dot-segment processing.

cases.name: double-dot-segment-with-consecutive-slashes
cases.rule: step 5: remove_dot_segments across a consecutive-slash boundary; /a//../b → /a/b
cases.$comment: remove_dot_segments treats // as 'segment + empty segment'; /../ pops the empty segment, leaving /a/b. Pins this behavior so parsers that treat // as a single boundary don't emit /b.

cases.name: empty-path-with-authority-becomes-slash
cases.rule: step 5: empty path with authority → /

cases.name: percent-encoded-hex-uppercased
cases.rule: step 6: uppercase %xx hex digits
cases.$comment: %2F is a reserved gen-delim and remains percent-encoded; only the hex case is normalized.

cases.name: percent-encoded-reserved-preserved
cases.rule: step 6: reserved characters remain percent-encoded; only hex case normalized
cases.$comment: %3A (':') and %3F ('?') are reserved sub-delims / gen-delims and MUST stay encoded. Implementers who over-decode produce an invalid path.

cases.name: percent-encoded-unreserved-tilde-decoded
cases.rule: step 6: decode percent-encoded unreserved '~' (RFC 3986 §2.3)
cases.$comment: %7E is the unreserved character '~'; RFC 3986 §6.2.2.2 requires decoding. Verifiers that leave it encoded fail step 10 on cross-SDK traffic.

cases.name: percent-encoded-unreserved-alpha-decoded
cases.rule: step 6: decode percent-encoded unreserved ALPHA (general rule, not just ~)
cases.$comment: %41 ('A') and %42 ('B') are unreserved ALPHA. Implementers commonly only decode %7E/%2D/%5F/%2E and miss the general rule — this case catches that bug.

cases.name: query-byte-preserved
cases.rule: step 7: preserve query string byte-for-byte (no reordering, no re-encoding)

cases.name: query-plus-not-decoded
cases.rule: step 7: '+' is preserved, NOT interpreted as space

cases.name: trailing-empty-query-preserved
cases.rule: step 7: trailing '?' with empty query preserved (distinct from no '?')

cases.name: no-query-preserved
cases.rule: step 7: URL with no '?' stays with no '?'

cases.name: fragment-stripped
cases.rule: step 8: strip fragment (RFC 9421 §2.2.2)

cases.name: malformed-port-without-host
cases.rule: step 3: authority with port but no host is malformed

cases.name: malformed-userinfo-without-host
cases.rule: step 3: authority with userinfo but no host is malformed

cases.name: malformed-empty-authority
cases.rule: step 3: empty authority is malformed

cases.name: malformed-ipv6-missing-closing-bracket
cases.rule: step 2: bracketed IPv6 host missing closing bracket is malformed

cases.name: malformed-bare-ipv6
cases.rule: step 2: IPv6 address outside brackets is malformed (ambiguous with port)
cases.$comment: Parsers variously treat '::1' as port or as host; rejecting unambiguously removes the interop divergence.

cases.name: malformed-ipv6-zone-identifier
cases.rule: step 2: IPv6 zone identifier (RFC 6874) is node-local and MUST be rejected in signed URLs
cases.reject_reason: IPv6 zone identifier in signed URL
cases.$comment: RFC 6874 §1 defines zone-ids as node-local; they have no meaning outside the signing host. A verifier on a different node cannot interpret them. Rejecting at the signer (MUST NOT sign) and verifier (MUST reject) closes the ambiguity.
